question:
Find a nonzero polynomial $P(x,y)$ such that $P(\lfloor a \rfloor,
\lfloor 2a \rfloor) = 0$ for all real numbers $a$.
(Note: $\lfloor \nu \rfloor$ is the greatest integer less than
or equal to $\nu$.)
solution:
Take $P(x,y) = (y-2x)(y-2x-1)$.
To see that this works, first note that if $m = \lfloor a \rfloor$,
then $2m$ is an integer less than or equal to $2a$, so
$2m \leq \lfloor 2a \rfloor$. On the other hand, $m+1$
is an integer strictly greater than $a$, so $2m+2$ is an integer
strictly greater than $2a$, so $\lfloor 2a \rfloor \leq 2m+1$.

descriptive_long.question:
Find a nonzero polynomial $polyvar(axisvar,ordinate)$ such that $polyvar(\lfloor alphanum \rfloor,
\lfloor 2 alphanum \rfloor) = 0$ for all real numbers $alphanum$.
(Note: $\lfloor greeknu \rfloor$ is the greatest integer less than
or equal to $greeknu$.)
descriptive_long.solution:
Take $polyvar(axisvar,ordinate) = (ordinate-2 axisvar)(ordinate-2 axisvar-1)$.
To see that this works, first note that if $integer = \lfloor alphanum \rfloor$,
then $2 integer$ is an integer less than or equal to $2 alphanum$, so
$2 integer \leq \lfloor 2 alphanum \rfloor$. On the other hand, $integer+1$
is an integer strictly greater than $alphanum$, so $2 integer+2$ is an integer
strictly greater than $2 alphanum$, so $\lfloor 2 alphanum \rfloor \leq 2 integer+1$.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Find a nonzero polynomial $landscape(pebblestone,windspinner)$ such that $landscape(\lfloor harborline \rfloor,
\lfloor 2harborline \rfloor) = 0$ for all real numbers $harborline$.
(Note: $\lfloor trellispost \rfloor$ is the greatest integer less than
or equal to $trellispost$.)
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Take $landscape(pebblestone,windspinner) = (windspinner-2pebblestone)(windspinner-2pebblestone-1)$.
To see that this works, first note that if $sandcastle = \lfloor harborline \rfloor$,
then $2sandcastle$ is an integer less than or equal to $2harborline$, so
$2sandcastle \leq \lfloor 2harborline \rfloor$. On the other hand, $sandcastle+1$
is an integer strictly greater than $harborline$, so $2sandcastle+2$ is an integer
strictly greater than $2harborline$, so $\lfloor 2harborline \rfloor \leq 2sandcastle+1$.}

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Find a nonzero polynomial $transcend(constant,fixedness)$ such that $transcend(\lfloor imaginary \rfloor,
\lfloor 2imaginary \rfloor) = 0$ for all real numbers $imaginary$.
(Note: $\lfloor ceilingval \rfloor$ is the greatest integer less than
or equal to $ceilingval$.)
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Take $transcend(constant,fixedness) = (fixedness-2constant)(fixedness-2constant-1)$.
To see that this works, first note that if $fraction = \lfloor imaginary \rfloor$,
then $2fraction$ is an integer less than or equal to $2imaginary$, so
$2fraction \leq \lfloor 2imaginary \rfloor$. On the other hand, $fraction+1$
is an integer strictly greater than $imaginary$, so $2fraction+2$ is an integer
strictly greater than $2imaginary$, so $\lfloor 2imaginary \rfloor \leq 2fraction+1$.}          nothing else ;)  \cdots   error? No extras.  Done.   /  Reviewed.   Chairman.       Ok.  Will deliver JSON only.  .     End.     CQ.  Finished.      <EOF>

garbled_string.question:
Find a nonzero polynomial $hjgrksla(bnmqvtcy,sfrpluad)$ such that $hjgrksla(\lfloor wkdjfzse \rfloor,
\lfloor 2wkdjfzse \rfloor) = 0$ for all real numbers $wkdjfzse$.
(Note: $\lfloor vchmzyad \rfloor$ is the greatest integer less than
or equal to $vchmzyad$.)
garbled_string.solution:
Take $hjgrksla(bnmqvtcy,sfrpluad) = (sfrpluad-2bnmqvtcy)(sfrpluad-2bnmqvtcy-1)$.
To see that this works, first note that if $tgxlnopr = \lfloor wkdjfzse \rfloor$,
then $2tgxlnopr$ is an integer less than or equal to $2wkdjfzse$, so
$2tgxlnopr \leq \lfloor 2wkdjfzse \rfloor$. On the other hand, $tgxlnopr+1$
is an integer strictly greater than $wkdjfzse$, so $2tgxlnopr+2$ is an integer
strictly greater than $2wkdjfzse$, so $\lfloor 2wkdjfzse \rfloor \leq 2tgxlnopr+1$.

kernel_variant.question:
Find a non-zero polynomial $P(x,y)$ with real coefficients such that
\[
P\! igl(\lfloor a\rfloor,\,\lfloor 5a\rfloor\bigr)=0\qquad\text{for every real }a.
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
Fix a \in  \mathbb{R} and let m = \lfloor a\rfloor . Then m \leq  a < m+1 implies 5m \leq  5a < 5m+5, so \lfloor 5a\rfloor  \in  {5m,5m+1,5m+2,5m+3,5m+4}. Setting x = m and y = \lfloor 5a\rfloor  gives y - 5x \in  {0,1,2,3,4}, and hence at least one factor of the product

    Q(x,y) = (y-5x)(y-5x-1)(y-5x-2)(y-5x-3)(y-5x-4)

vanishes, so Q(\lfloor a\rfloor ,\lfloor 5a\rfloor ) = 0 for all a. Multiplying by any nonzero constant preserves this property; for example,

    P(x,y) = 7\cdot Q(x,y) = 7\prod _{j=0}^{4}(y-5x-j)

is a nonzero polynomial with P(\lfloor a\rfloor ,\lfloor 5a\rfloor ) = 0 for every real a.

core_steps: Set m = ‚Ćäa‚Ćč so that m ‚Č§ a < m+1.
Conclude ‚Ćä2a‚Ćč ‚ąą {2m, 2m+1}, i.e. y‚ąí2x is either 0 or 1 when x = ‚Ćäa‚Ćč and y = ‚Ćä2a‚Ćč.
Choose a non-zero polynomial that vanishes for y‚ąí2x = 0 or 1, e.g. (y‚ąí2x)(y‚ąí2x‚ąí1).

slot1.description: The multiplying factor 2 used with a (in both ‚Ćä2a‚Ćč and the term 2x) can be any positive integer k; the argument works identically with k in place of 2.

slot2.description: Corresponding linear factors: for general k the polynomial may be ‚ąŹ_{j=0}^{k‚ąí1}(y‚ąíkx‚ąíj); when k=2 this is (y‚ąí2x)(y‚ąí2x‚ąí1). Thus the number of consecutive factors (and the final constant 1) adapts to k.

slot3.description: An overall non-zero constant multiplier of the polynomial does not affect the vanishing property.
